Precision Widgets: Catalog and Buying Guide

Choosing a precision widget starts with the tolerance class your line actually needs. Over-specifying costs money; under-specifying costs yield. This guide compares the three common tolerance classes, the materials they are machined from, and the drift characteristics we measured across 400 hours of thermal cycling in our lab. Each section below is self-contained, so you can jump straight to the class you are evaluating.

Tolerance classes explained

Class A widgets hold one micron across the full operating range. They are machined from stabilized alloy and individually certified. Class B widgets hold five microns and suit most general production lines. Class C widgets are the economy tier and hold twenty microns, which is acceptable for non-critical fixturing but not for metrology work.

Materials and drift

Stabilized alloy drifts the least in our testing, at under half a micron per hundred thermal cycles. Hard-anodized aluminum is lighter and cheaper but drifts roughly four times faster. Polymer-composite bodies should only be used where mass matters more than repeatability.

Maintenance planner

Re-calibrate Class A units every ninety days, Class B every one hundred eighty days, and Class C annually. Log every calibration in the tracker so interval drift becomes visible before it becomes scrap.

Frequently asked questions

How often should widgets be recalibrated? See the planner above — the interval depends on tolerance class. What voids the certification? Any disassembly of the sensor cartridge voids the individual certificate.
